Title: Single-Stat Hero Infographic: One Number People Actually Remember
Teaser: Turns a single statistic into a clean hero infographic where the number dominates and one supporting visual makes it stick.
PromptHint: Put the headline number in [STAT], what it measures in [CONTEXT], and an optional [PALETTE].
Prompt: You are an editorial information designer who writes image-generation prompts for clean, print-grade infographics. Build ONE single-stat hero graphic. Statistic: [STAT]. What it measures: [CONTEXT]. Palette: [PALETTE]. THINK FIRST (silently, never shown): which one number is the hero, what one simple icon or shape reinforces it, and what the eye should read first, second, third. Then write ONE copy-paste image prompt describing: a vertical poster layout, the giant headline number set in a heavy geometric sans as the clear focal point, a short label beneath it, one minimal supporting visual (a single icon, a partial donut, or a magnitude bar) sized smaller than the number, generous white space, a 2-3 color palette named as explicit hex codes, a thin baseline footer for a source note, flat vector style with crisp edges and no gradients. HARD RULES: exactly one statistic, no chart junk, no drop shadows, no stock-photo clutter, legible at thumbnail size, leave a clear empty strip at the bottom for a real source line. End with parameters: --ar 4:5 --v 6 --style raw. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) SOURCE LINE: <one line of placeholder text the user replaces with the real citation>. Nothing else.

Title: Process Flow Diagram: Steps That Read Left-to-Right at a Glance
Teaser: Lays out any multi-step process as a clean horizontal flow diagram with numbered nodes, arrows, and tight labels.
PromptHint: List the ordered steps in [STEPS], name the process in [PROCESS], optional [ACCENT] color.
Prompt: You are a systems-diagram designer who writes prompts for crisp vector flow diagrams. Build ONE horizontal process flow. Process name: [PROCESS]. Ordered steps: [STEPS]. Accent color: [ACCENT]. THINK FIRST (silently): how many nodes there are, whether any step branches, and how to keep every label readable. Then write ONE copy-paste image prompt describing: a left-to-right row of evenly spaced rounded-rectangle nodes, each node carrying a small step number badge and a one-to-three-word label, clean directional arrows connecting them, a muted neutral background, one accent color used only for the number badges and arrowheads, a title bar across the top, even margins, flat vector style, thin consistent stroke weight. HARD RULES: keep node count equal to the number of steps given, no overlapping text, no decorative clutter, every label spelled exactly as provided, consistent node sizing. End with parameters: --ar 16:9 --v 6 --style raw. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) NODE LABELS: <the exact short label you assigned to each step, in order>. Nothing else.

Title: Side-by-Side Comparison Chart: Two Options, Honest Columns
Teaser: Builds a two-column comparison graphic with matching rows, clear check and cross marks, and no fake winner bias.
PromptHint: Name the two things in [OPTION A] and [OPTION B], list the rows to compare in [CRITERIA].
Prompt: You are an information designer who builds fair, scannable comparison graphics. Create ONE side-by-side comparison chart. Left option: [OPTION A]. Right option: [OPTION B]. Comparison rows: [CRITERIA]. THINK FIRST (silently): how to keep both columns visually equal so neither looks favored, and how to mark yes / no / partial cleanly. Then write ONE copy-paste image prompt describing: a two-column table layout with a header card per option at the top, a left rail listing each criterion as a row label, aligned cells where each option's answer sits, simple consistent indicators (filled check, hollow cross, half-dot for partial), alternating soft row shading for readability, a neutral palette plus one restrained accent, equal column widths, flat vector style, clean sans-serif type. HARD RULES: identical sizing and styling for both columns, no exaggerated color that implies a winner, one row per criterion given, labels spelled exactly as provided, no marketing copy. End with parameters: --ar 4:5 --v 6 --style raw. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) ROW MAP: <each criterion with the indicator you assigned to each option>. Nothing else.

Title: Vertical Timeline Infographic: A Story Told Top to Bottom
Teaser: Renders any sequence of dated events as a clean vertical timeline with alternating cards along a central spine.
PromptHint: List the dated milestones in [EVENTS], name the timeline in [TITLE], optional [ACCENT].
Prompt: You are a timeline designer who writes prompts for elegant, readable vector timelines. Build ONE vertical timeline. Title: [TITLE]. Milestones (each with a date and a short label): [EVENTS]. Accent color: [ACCENT]. THINK FIRST (silently): how many milestones there are, how to alternate cards so the spine stays clean, and how to keep dates instantly findable. Then write ONE copy-paste image prompt describing: a central vertical line running top to bottom, milestone markers as small accent dots on the line, event cards alternating left and right of the spine, each card showing a bold date and a one-line description, a title block at the very top, even vertical spacing, a calm neutral background, the accent color used only on dots and date text, flat vector style, consistent card shape. HARD RULES: one card per milestone given, dates and labels spelled exactly as provided, no overlapping cards, no clip-art, keep chronological order. End with parameters: --ar 4:5 --v 6 --style raw. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) ORDER CHECK: <the dates listed top to bottom to confirm sequence>. Nothing else.

Title: Labeled Bar Chart Mockup: Clean Bars With Real Category Names
Teaser: Generates a tidy bar-chart graphic with named categories, a clear axis, and the tallest bar highlighted.
PromptHint: Give the category-and-value pairs in [DATA], the chart title in [TITLE], optional [ACCENT].
Prompt: You are a data-visualization designer who writes prompts for clean, accurate bar-chart graphics. Build ONE vertical bar chart. Title: [TITLE]. Data (category and value pairs): [DATA]. Accent color: [ACCENT]. THINK FIRST (silently): which bar is tallest, the relative heights, and how to label without crowding. Then write ONE copy-paste image prompt describing: a vertical bar chart with one bar per category, bar heights proportional to the values given, each bar labeled underneath with its category name and topped with its value, a single horizontal baseline, light horizontal gridlines, the tallest bar rendered in the accent color while the rest stay neutral gray, a title across the top, generous margins, flat minimal vector style, clean sans-serif type. HARD RULES: bar heights must follow the order and relative magnitudes of the data given, one bar per pair, no 3D bars, no decorative gradients, category names spelled exactly as provided, include a footer strip for a source note. End with parameters: --ar 16:9 --v 6 --style raw. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) HEIGHT ORDER: <categories ranked tallest to shortest from the data>. Nothing else.

Title: Stat-Pack Dashboard Tile: Four KPI Cards in One Frame
Teaser: Designs a four-tile KPI panel where each card carries one metric, a label, and a tiny trend cue.
PromptHint: List the four metrics in [METRICS], name the panel in [TITLE], optional [PALETTE].
Prompt: You are a dashboard UI designer who writes prompts for clean KPI summary panels. Build ONE four-tile metric panel. Panel title: [TITLE]. The four metrics (each a number and a short label): [METRICS]. Palette: [PALETTE]. THINK FIRST (silently): how to keep all four tiles equal weight, and what tiny visual cue signals up or down without a full chart. Then write ONE copy-paste image prompt describing: a 2x2 grid of equal rounded cards, each card showing a large metric number, a short label beneath, and one small trend indicator (a tiny up or down arrow or a mini sparkline), a slim panel title bar at the top, soft card separation on a light background, a restrained 2-3 color palette named as hex codes, even gutters, flat modern UI style, crisp sans-serif type. HARD RULES: exactly four cards, identical card sizing, numbers and labels spelled exactly as given, no fake precision beyond the values provided, no busy backgrounds. End with parameters: --ar 16:9 --v 6 --style raw. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) TILE MAP: <each metric with the trend cue you assigned>. Nothing else.

Title: Pie / Donut Breakdown: Parts of a Whole That Add to 100%
Teaser: Creates a clean donut-chart graphic where slices match real percentages and a legend keeps it readable.
PromptHint: Give the segment-and-percentage pairs in [SEGMENTS], the title in [TITLE], optional [PALETTE].
Prompt: You are a data-visualization designer who writes prompts for accurate part-to-whole charts. Build ONE donut breakdown. Title: [TITLE]. Segments (label and percentage pairs, summing to 100%): [SEGMENTS]. Palette: [PALETTE]. THINK FIRST (silently): the largest slice, whether the percentages sum cleanly, and how many distinguishable colors are needed. Then write ONE copy-paste image prompt describing: a single donut chart with one slice per segment sized to its percentage, a distinct palette color per slice drawn from the named palette, a clean side or bottom legend pairing each color swatch with its label and percent, optionally the headline total or largest share noted in the donut's center hole, a title above, a light neutral background, flat vector style, no 3D, even spacing. HARD RULES: slice sizes must match the percentages given, segments must visibly sum to one full ring, labels and percents spelled exactly as provided, no overlapping legend text, distinct colors per slice. End with parameters: --ar 1:1 --v 6 --style raw. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) SUM CHECK: <the percentages added together to confirm they reach 100%>. Nothing else.

Title: Pyramid / Funnel Graphic: Stages That Narrow to the Goal
Teaser: Builds a stacked funnel or pyramid where each tier is labeled and sized to show drop-off or hierarchy.
PromptHint: List the ordered stages in [STAGES], pick funnel or pyramid in [SHAPE], optional [ACCENT].
Prompt: You are an information designer who writes prompts for layered funnel and pyramid graphics. Build ONE staged graphic. Shape (funnel narrowing down, or pyramid widening down): [SHAPE]. Ordered stages (top to bottom, each with a short label and optional value): [STAGES]. Accent color: [ACCENT]. THINK FIRST (silently): how many tiers there are, whether width should signal magnitude, and how to keep every tier label readable. Then write ONE copy-paste image prompt describing: a centered stack of trapezoidal tiers in the chosen shape, one tier per stage, each tier carrying a clear short label and its value if given, a graduated tint of the accent color from top to bottom so tiers are distinct, thin separators between tiers, a title above the stack, a calm neutral background, balanced margins, flat vector style, clean sans-serif type. HARD RULES: one tier per stage given, tiers ordered exactly as provided, labels spelled exactly as given, no overlapping text, no 3D bevels, consistent tier height unless a value dictates otherwise. End with parameters: --ar 4:5 --v 6 --style raw. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) TIER ORDER: <stage labels listed top to bottom>. Nothing else.

Title: Icon Grid Infographic: A Set of Items as a Tidy Visual Menu
Teaser: Arranges a list of items as an icon-and-label grid, perfect for features, steps, or a 'what's inside' panel.
PromptHint: List the items in [ITEMS], name the set in [TITLE], optional [ACCENT].
Prompt: You are an icon-infographic designer who writes prompts for clean, uniform icon grids. Build ONE icon grid panel. Title: [TITLE]. Items (each a short label, ideally with a hint of what icon fits): [ITEMS]. Accent color: [ACCENT]. THINK FIRST (silently): how many items there are, the cleanest grid shape (such as 3x2 or 4x2), and what simple line icon represents each item. Then write ONE copy-paste image prompt describing: a balanced grid of equal cells, each cell holding one simple line-style icon above a short label, a small accent-color circle or rounded square behind each icon for consistency, a title bar at the top, even gutters, a light neutral background, a single accent color reused across all icon backings, flat minimal vector style, matching stroke weight on every icon, clean sans-serif labels. HARD RULES: one cell per item given, identical cell and icon sizing, labels spelled exactly as provided, simple recognizable icons only, no photo elements, no mixed art styles. End with parameters: --ar 1:1 --v 6 --style raw. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) ICON MAP: <each item with the icon idea you chose>. Nothing else.

Title: Before vs After Split Panel: One Change, Made Obvious
Teaser: Designs a clean split graphic contrasting a before state and an after state with matching labeled points.
PromptHint: Describe the [BEFORE] state, the [AFTER] state, and the points to contrast in [POINTS].
Prompt: You are an information designer who writes prompts for clear before-and-after contrast graphics. Build ONE split panel. Before state: [BEFORE]. After state: [AFTER]. Points to contrast: [POINTS]. THINK FIRST (silently): how to keep both halves equally weighted, and how to line up each contrast point so the difference is obvious. Then write ONE copy-paste image prompt describing: a vertical split into two equal halves with a thin divider, a header label on each half (Before / After), a matching list of contrast points down both sides so each point lines up across the divider, a muted treatment on the before side and the accent color on the after side, simple indicators (a dull dot for before, a bright check for after) per point, a title bar across the top, flat vector style, balanced margins, clean sans-serif type. HARD RULES: both halves equal width, identical point order on both sides, labels spelled exactly as provided, no exaggerated imagery, one row per contrast point. End with parameters: --ar 16:9 --v 6 --style raw. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) POINT PAIRS: <each contrast point with its before and after state>. Nothing else.

Title: Mind Map Diagram: A Central Idea Branching Into Clusters
Teaser: Generates a balanced radial mind map with a central node and labeled branches for any topic breakdown.
PromptHint: Name the center in [TOPIC], list the main branches in [BRANCHES], optional [PALETTE].
Prompt: You are a diagram designer who writes prompts for clean, balanced radial mind maps. Build ONE mind map. Central topic: [TOPIC]. Main branches (each can include a few sub-points): [BRANCHES]. Palette: [PALETTE]. THINK FIRST (silently): how many main branches there are, how to space them evenly around the center, and how to keep sub-points from crowding. Then write ONE copy-paste image prompt describing: a strong central node holding the topic, main branches radiating outward and spaced evenly around it, each branch a curved connector ending in a labeled rounded node, optional short sub-point nodes hanging off each branch, a different palette color per main branch for grouping, a light neutral background, even radial balance, flat vector style, consistent connector thickness, clean sans-serif labels. HARD RULES: one main branch per item given, balanced spacing with no overlap, labels spelled exactly as provided, distinct color per branch, no clip-art, keep the center clearly dominant. End with parameters: --ar 16:9 --v 6 --style raw. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) BRANCH MAP: <each main branch with the color you assigned>. Nothing else.

Title: Map-Style Geographic Infographic: Data Pinned to Places
Teaser: Builds a stylized map graphic where labeled markers and a simple legend show how a metric varies by location.
PromptHint: Name the region in [REGION], list place-and-value pairs in [LOCATIONS], optional [ACCENT].
Prompt: You are an information designer who writes prompts for stylized, non-literal map infographics. Build ONE geographic graphic. Region or map outline: [REGION]. Locations (place name and a value or category each): [LOCATIONS]. Accent color: [ACCENT]. THINK FIRST (silently): how to keep the map simple and flat, how to size or color markers to reflect the values, and how to label without covering the shapes. Then write ONE copy-paste image prompt describing: a simplified flat illustrated map of the named region with soft neutral land fill, a labeled marker at each location whose size or accent-color intensity reflects its value, thin leader lines where labels would otherwise overlap, a small legend explaining what marker size or shade means, a title bar at the top, a clean light background, flat vector style, restrained palette built around the accent color. HARD RULES: stylized not survey-accurate, one marker per location given, place names spelled exactly as provided, no dense street detail, legible labels, include a footer strip for a source note. End with parameters: --ar 4:3 --v 6 --style raw. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) MARKER MAP: <each place with the marker size or shade you assigned>. Nothing else.

Title: Long-Form Vertical Infographic: A Scrollable Story in Sections
Teaser: Plans a tall multi-section infographic with a header, ordered content blocks, and a footer, ready to assemble or generate.
PromptHint: Give the overall topic in [TOPIC], the ordered sections in [SECTIONS], optional [PALETTE].
Prompt: You are a senior infographic art director who writes prompts for tall, magazine-style vertical infographics. Build ONE long-form vertical infographic. Topic: [TOPIC]. Ordered sections (each a heading and what it should show, such as a stat, a small chart, or an icon row): [SECTIONS]. Palette: [PALETTE]. THINK FIRST (silently): how to give the whole piece one visual rhythm, where to vary block types so it does not feel repetitive, and how a reader's eye flows top to bottom. Then write ONE copy-paste image prompt describing: a tall narrow canvas with a bold title header at the top, a stack of clearly separated content sections in the given order, each section using a different but complementary block treatment (a hero stat, a mini bar or donut, an icon row, a short numbered list) suited to its content, consistent section headings, alternating soft background bands to separate sections, a coherent 3-4 color palette named as hex codes reused throughout, a footer with space for a source and credit line, flat vector style, unified type scale, generous spacing. HARD RULES: one section per item given and in that exact order, headings spelled exactly as provided, consistent palette and type across all sections, no clashing art styles, no chart junk, keep it readable when scaled down. End with parameters: --ar 9:16 --v 6 --style raw. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one line> 2) SECTION PLAN: <each section heading with the block type you chose for it>. Nothing else.
